speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three, Mr. Walter Absil. Mr. Absil, please continue with your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had told you how we broke into our little house and got some valuables and some, some clothing out of there. And I-- by night, we walked. It's very dangerous to walk back to my uncle's house. Next day, I told my sister to stay where she was. She was visiting an aunt in the countryside. And from there and then, with the help of Belgium friends, I obtained a place for her in the convent school of Waterloo, the famous Waterloo of the battle and Napoleon. And she checked in there as a student and survived the rest of the, the war in that convent, even though part of the convent was occupied by German troops. I myself with the help, again, of Belgian resistance and underground, was told to take a certain street car. Belgium in those days was crisscrossed by what's called in Flemish a [FLEMISH], a farmer's train. And it really was a, an electrical street car that operated like a train and went from village to village. And they said-- they told me to take that street car to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to get in touch with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really wouldn't recall that. I tried to, to remember. But probably, the landlady that rented us the little house that was instrumental in getting my sister-- she was friends with the Mother Superior-- getting my sister into the convent school. She probably gave me some names. But I really don't recall. But I do know I got strict instructions to hold my suitcase in the same hand with the German edition of a type of magazine. It was called And it had all the battles and all the victories of the Germans in there with lots of pictures. And I did that. And at the stop in [INAUDIBLE], which is in a little village near the French border, a young man accosted me and said, today, we cannot go any further. You sleep here in this hotel, right, right across the street. And I did. Next morning, he picked me up and said that he would go-- this is, again, the street car-- the front platform, he would get on. And I would get on the rear platform. And when he got off, I should get off. And that's how I got to a little village right next to the French border. And the, the old lady-- there was an old lady there. And her son was an accountant in the village. And I had my room. And I paid room and board. And I came there with 3,000 francs. And eventually, I started bringing tobacco to the Polish prisoners in France. And, and by the time I was liberated a year later, after paying my room and board, I still had 3,000 francs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the name of this village. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was [INAUDIBLE]. It's a tiny little village. It only was a church with, with one street going north-south on one street going east-west. And that was all there was to it. I'd have to look it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Polish prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get there from Poland? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were families. They were not prisoners in the sense that they were, that they were only men. They were, they were really families-- women, children. And they were working the fields in northern France. They-- the Germans had used them like slave labor. And I remember that I used to play cards with them. They still own me 150 zlotys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out which part of Poland they came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have idea. They mostly did-- spoke only Polish. And while I did learn a few words, I wasn't versed enough to speak Polish and find out where they came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically-- how-- first of all, how were you able to get in touch with them if they were prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because there was, there, they, they worked the fields. And I got a job working there, too. I didn't work for-- there was one, one German officer there. But I had nothing to do with him. Somehow, I got a job. And I got paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German officer didn't care that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: He supervised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talking to the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't care. And I even talked German to him. And he says, well, how come you speak German? I said, well, I'm from [INAUDIBLE]. And I speak German. I spoke to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He never suspected you as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: He never asked me never suspected me. But the Poles suspect me, too. The Poles tried to pull my pants down to verify that I'm a Jew. But the German officer never did. He had been injured in the Russian front. And this was a cushy job for him to supervise a bunch of Polish prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your knowledge, the, the Polish prisoners never wanted to report you as a Jew to the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess they didn't. And they didn't. And otherwise, I might have gotten into trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see other Germans in the countryside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was the only one. And he was only in France. He never came to, to, to Belgium to cross the border. I guess he was in charge of this labor camp, if you want to call it. They were not in-- they were not locked up. There were no barbed wires, nothing-- a farm where they lived. I guess they could have run away. But where would they go?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from here?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean from the farm in--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the farm-- I recall three, four, five different places. Now, why did I move around? I really don't know. I guess this place where, where the accountants had no more work for me or somebody else requested my-- every farm in that area had somebody hiding because the Belgian youth, the, the boys, were supposed to go to forced labor in Germany. And when they didn't want to go, they were hiding on farms. And I had fake ID. I called myself Emile Blondy, born in [INAUDIBLE]. And with that ID, I passed for a Belgian. The people that hid me never knew I was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get your fake ID?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the resistance people gave it to me. They, they made it up for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how, how that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just met somebody in that little village and came and took the information that they wanted. He wanted to know my, my birthday. And my exact birthday is on that fake card.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pay for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It was free of charge. I didn't pay anything. They gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in touch with the resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in touch with the resistance. They were all over the place in the countryside. And the day before the, the Americans marched in, they said, would you come and help us if we have to fight the Germans? But that never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever seen them in action? No. No. The only thing, once again, I was hidden at one farm. And the resistance had burned the telephone wire of the Germans. So the Germans, instead of taking hostages and shooting them, the officer went to the, the mayor of that little place and says, listen. From now on, I want you to, to watch those wires. I don't want the resistance to burn my telephone wires anymore. Otherwise, I don't know what. So I was selected as one of the males in the village, that this was my, my war effort for the German army to, at night, we had to watch that the wires would not be burned again or cut. So what we did-- it was cold, freezing cold. And we had built the fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in order for the fire not to be extinguished by the wind, we had built kind of a wall made out of straw-- you know, the, the compressed straw that the farmers have in the field. We made a little wall with that. And of course, our wall started burning. And we nearly burned down the wires ourselves. But we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you selected for this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just selected young people at random to, to watch for the-- I, I was a resident that they didn't know I was a Jew. I had a-- I was hiding. I had fake ID. But the, the people in charge of the village says, well, there's a guy. Let him watch the wires, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: According to your knowledge, did anyone ever get caught during this time, any Belgians or Jews hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One day, I came down in my slippers. And the old lady serving me my coffee said, don't go out today. The village three kilometers down the road was surrounded by the Germans. And they arrested some British flyboys-- you know, shot down British aviators. And they arrested all the people that were hiding in that village. And they all around. They're just around the corner. So don't go out. Stay in here. While I was having my coffee, somebody knocks at the door. And I see, through the milky glass, I see kind of a, a pointy hat. And I says, uh-uh, the Germans are here. I run out the back door. I jumped one of those big hedges that they have in France and, and Belgium. I don't know if you ever heard of them. Those hedges stopped the tanks of the American army and-- till somebody invented a railroad. They kind of lifted the hedges out of the way. You couldn't, you couldn't-- I, I jumped that hedge just like a flash. And I run towards the forest where I had a friend in a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw the German patrol going up and down the road. And at the-- and I, I laid low till they walked by. And I went to that farm and says, listen, the Germans came to get me. Would you please go and get my shoes? I only have my slippers. So he took his bicycle. And he went to my place. He came back with my shoes. He says, you stupid [INAUDIBLE]. The Germans didn't come to get you. The neighbor warned you not to go out. Where you are, the Germans are not. But here, this is surrounded by the Germans. So I put my shoes on and shoved my slippers in my pocket and waited till the patrol came down the road again and went back home. I tried to jump that hedge. I could never jump that hedge again. That was the excitement of that day. You asked me if somebody got arrested. Yes, people got arrested. What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. The Germans dragged them away. You didn't ask questions then. You just laid low.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other close calls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I-- once leaving after my parents were arrested, I now had no ID card. That-- yes, I had the fake ID card that the underground gave me. But I had no real ID card. And in order to obtain ration cards and be able to buy bread or sugar or anything, you needed a ration card. So I had made an application. And I told them that I had lost my papers. And I had that application in my pocket, along with my fake ID, of course. When I came out of a park, there was a German patrol of military police-- not Gestapo, but military police. They, they wore a big metal plate on their, on their bellies and on their, on their chest. And they pointed the machine gun on my belly button. And the first thing they would say is [INAUDIBLE]. And the next thing is [NON-ENGLISH], your papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I took out my papers. And thanks God, he couldn't read French and didn't realize that I had two different names. And the next thing they did, they were kind of searching me. And they found a round object in my pocket. And when he took it out, it was a ping pong ball. So he started laughing and told the young lady that was with him, should we arrest him or should we let him go? And, and he says, get out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one close call. And the other one was when I, I hiked a ride back to my hideout near the French border. And I was on top of a, a truck that had big wooden crates going to Charleroi. Charleroi is the center for coal in Belgium, and also for glass. And they were, they were getting glass panes in Charleroi. And I sat on those empty crates and when we came to a railroad crossing. And, and the train went by. And we sat maybe third car in line when the British dive bombers attacked that train. And I came down from my crate pretty fast and went into the ditch with the German soldiers. They were running just the same. They went into the ditch. And there were a lot of people killed on the train that morning. It probably was only a civilian train. But before the D-day, the Britishes attacked anything that moved. They bombed anything that moved. Any train, any transport was, was attacked. And that day, I escaped narrowly again. I told you about Indiana Jones of the Holocaust. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were hiding, did you have any news of your other relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I, I wrote my own uncle. And I believe that when I was stuck at that railroad crossing, I had visited my uncle. That must have been very, very close to D-Day, June 6th, 1944. And I had visited him. And I knew that my sister was still in the convent. The rest-- I believed that my parents would come back. All through the war, I believed that they were in Holland and they would be back, liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening in the east?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not know. We did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no idea at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely not. Nobody knew in the occupied countries what the Germans did. I certainly didn't have any knowledge. I had no contact with any outsiders.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hiding. This was the last farm where you were hiding. Or did you go to another farm after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the, the last farm was the biggest farm where I was, where I I was liberated. And in that-- that was a good farm. The reason it was a good farm, I had a companion. There was another boy, again, a fellow from [INAUDIBLE], I think, that was hiding there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know the location of this farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I know the location of that farm, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was right near [INAUDIBLE], on the outskirts of [INAUDIBLE] and near the French border.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to this last farm? What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't be able to tell you that. But I would say for about three, three months, I was on that big farm where we had a lot of animals. And we had a lot of fields, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the owner of the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. They, again, there, there, there was an old man. And there was a couple of young Belgians with which we joked and laughed. And we had a good time

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he take you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, don't forget that a lot of Belgians were in the war. They were prisoners of war in, in Germany. So you needed labor for your fields. And we were free, no charge. All you had to do is feed us. So we worked the fields. We, we milked the cows. And even though the Belgians were very nice to them, we were the Belgians hiding from the Germans. And I think it was to their advantage. And if they would have found out I was Jewish, I don't think it would have made a big difference. But I didn't rock the boat. I didn't tell them. Only the old lady next to the church was mad at me for not going to church on Sunday, which was stupid of my part. But I was 19 years old. And I, I was invincible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that boy you were hiding with?

speaker: interviewee
text: You got me. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on this farm until liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the liberation was very funny. Because again, I took my bicycle. And I was riding in the forest when I encountered this crazy vehicle I had never seen before with two soldiers with different type of helmets. And they asked me in English, are there any Germans here? I didn't understand English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, we got to understand each other and say [NON-ENGLISH]. There are no Germans here. And they went. And I was liberated. I went back home and said, listen, we're liberated. The guy says, where? I don't see anything. We're not liberated. I said, yeah, yeah. I saw Americans. They say, you must be dreaming. I said, I'm going home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you can't go home. We've got the fields. We've got to bring in the, the crops. I said, I don't give a shit about your crops. I'm going home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next morning, my bicycle had a flat by miracle. I had to fix my flat. Then I took my little suitcase. And I pedaled into [INAUDIBLE]. Did you see any other Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: That day in [INAUDIBLE], the, the army went through in force. You had tanks and trucks and motorcycles and people yelling and screaming. Then I really was liberated the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know which division of the American army this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, no. But I know that I could only take a ride with them till Charleroi again, where they turned towards the German border, towards [INAUDIBLE], while I was going the other way towards Brussels. Again, I hiked all ride. But this time, it wasn't the army. It was some kind of truck. I hiked a ride. And I arrived at my uncle's place two days after Brussels had been liberated. And the people were still dancing in the streets. It was pandemonium.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that-- I can only imagine what it was like to feel that you were finally liberated. But I'd like to hear you tell me how you felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like, like it was, it was like an anticlimax. After four years of hiding, after four years of, you know, outwitting the Germans and playing cat and mouse, and suddenly a jeep goes by, doesn't-- only stop for a second to ask me a question. And I'm liberated. It, it, it felt really funny. Only when I went into [INAUDIBLE] and I really saw American troops and, and flags and yelling and screaming that I felt liberated. And then I wanted to go home as fast as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any German prisoners on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not, no, no, really no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole time, not one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not one. I believe that the Germans pulled back from that area and very fast. That, that also accounts for the call of the underground that wanted me to come and help fight the Germans. And there was nobody to fight. I would say they pulled back very fast from that area. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the underground after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: You only saw them parade in the street. But the people that I dealt with, I didn't see them again. I, I got out of there fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Brussels now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing that you did as a free man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I first moved into my uncle's house. And he had a few-- I had given him all, all my belongings before I left. And there was a, a little bit of money left. My sister came out of the convent school. My grandmother still lived in the old place. She never had a day's problems with the, with the Germans. And I had to support them. So I had to find a job. That's what I did. I started selling labels. And first of all, I started selling anything you could find. I remember blouses and rubber balls and watches and whatever you could find. Everything was at a premium. And everything was in short supply.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when somebody gave you a sample of something, you went to somebody else. You want to buy this? They said yes. You went back to the first guy. And the goods were gone. So that's that's how you try to earn a living. I worked-- Did you go back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. That's funny. Paul asked me-- my grandson asked me, how come you didn't go back to school? I had to earn a living. I certainly didn't have time to go back to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a young man now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 20 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do as a 20-year-old man? How, how did you socialize?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to the Austrian social club where people came out of the camps, young people came out of the camps. Some had lost their wives. Some had lost their husbands. Other youngsters had survived, like me, and came out of the, the hiding places all over the place. And that's where I met my wife-to-be. And that's where we went. We socialized, like you say. We went to picnics in the parks and in the forest. And we were all young Austrians. And I guess everybody got married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in 1945, which was only one year after-- November, 1945-- a year and a few months after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Erika Erika.

speaker: interviewer
text: You established a home in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: We established a home in Brussels, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. We had two children. First, we had Ingrid, who was born in 1946. And then we had Michael, who was born in 1949. And in 1951, we applied to immigrate to Australia or the United States and to Canada. And Canada won. They got us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to your journey to Canada, Mr. Absil, I would like you to explain one thing to me. Why is it that the Belgians were so different in the way they behaved toward Jews from, let's say, Germans or Austrians? How would you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had, in Europe, you had some people. Even though the Belgians are Catholic and most of the central European Catholics were terrible anti-Semites, the Belgians were not anti-Semitic. They also hated the Germans. Because in the First World War, the Germans really behaved atrociously. They raped women and, and, and killed. And they really hated them. They called them [NON-ENGLISH]. That stems from the inscription on the, the, the headlights of the German trucks. Bosch is an electrical outfit that still exists today. And the, the Belgians saw this Bosch. And they, they made it an insult. If you couldn't be in Denmark, where the Danes saved all their Jews-- they ferried them. They, they declared-- they closed the university. They, they, they had a general strike and used that time to ferry all their Jews to Sweden in little boats. Only 500 of the 7,000 Danish Jews were arrested by the Germans. They went to Theresienstadt. And the Danes with Red Cross buses went to get them. They got them out of Theresienstadt. With the exception of a few Jews that died in Theresienstadt, all the Danish Jews were being saved by their neighbors. The homes and the houses of the Jews in Denmark were kept intact by their Danish neighbors. They went in there every week. And they dusted the apartments. When the Jews came back after the war, they had everything. All their belongings were intact. If you couldn't be in Denmark, Belgium wasn't a bad place. The Belgians did their utmost to save a lot of children. They had orphanages. They had-- in different cloisters, they had Jews hidden, just like my sister. I don't think anybody knew, except the Mother Superior, that my sister was Jewish. And in the countryside, because of all the, the ill feeling against the Germans, the Belgians saved us and saved a lot of other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Holland, they are hiding behind Anne Frank. The largest amount of the Jewish population in any Western country was arrested in Holland. In France, the Vichy police or-- you know that? On the, on the demands of the Germans, the Germans asked for 40,000 Jews. The Vichy police picked up about 16,000 Jews from Paris and locked them up for a week in the [NON-ENGLISH], which is a sports arena, without food, without water, without facilities. They then separated the men from the women. They, they deported the men first to Drancy, which was an assembly camp on the outskirts of Paris, and from there to Auschwitz. They then sent the mothers. And the, the French were left with three and 1/2 thousand children alone. And they asked the Germans, don't you want to take the children? Because first, it said from 16 to 40, nobody under that. And the Germans says, we can't take the children unless we get permission from Berlin. So the French again says, can't just take the children? So on the urgings of the French, eventually they got permission from Berlin. And they stuffed three and 1/2 thousand little children into railroad cars and sent them to Auschwitz, where they were gassed immediately. After this became known, there was kind of a backlash. And the small clergy and some Frenchmen started to help. There's a village in France that's called [NON-ENGLISH] something. And this village of 5,000 people is inhabited by Huguenots. You know, who Huguenots? And they're Protestant. And they were discriminated and killed in the Middle Ages. And they remembered what happened to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, we want to save 5000 Jews and did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Imagine if the rest of the Christian world of Europe had acted like that, if every little village of 5,000 people had saved 5,000 souls, how many millions could have been saved? Mr. Absil, you are now in the fortunate situation of being able to go to the new world, to go to Canada. When did that day come when you actually left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we left-- we, we obtained the visa, an immigration visa, in, in the fall of 1951. We had to undergo medical [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inspections, you know, what they did. They tested our lungs. And we got x-rays taken. And, and we finally embarked from [INAUDIBLE].
